1) Once you’ve surrounded a Shrieker with wool, break it. If the Warden ain’t around, the sensors can’t really do anything on their own. 
2) Check third person mode RIGOROUSLY. Nothing sucks more than getting blindsided (ha ha) by a Shrieker that you couldn’t see. 
3) Set sneak to “Toggle.” Your fingers will thank you, trust me. 
4) *There can be multiple Wardens active if you set off multiple Shriekers,* so be VERY careful when you’re in an area where there’s more than one present. 
5) You have 3 shrieks before the Warden shows its blind face. 
6) However, if you break a Shrieker as it’s shrieking, it will instantly summon the Warden REGARDLESS of your current warning level. I found this out the hard way, and nearly had a fucking heart attack. 
7) The Warden can smell you if you’re less than 16 blocks away from it. 
8) DO NOT PANIC. Panic causes mistakes, and mistakes cause death.

Pennies have no ridges and feel flat, nickels have none, but they have a thick rimming so you can feel the sides, quarters have very detectable ridges, and dimes have scraper-like ridges. It takes about 10 minutes to learn to feel the difference between them, but it is occasionally useful

Another one I would add, although it applies to right hand fingering in general, is to keep the guitar face more vertical. As a beginner it is natural to tip the lower edge of guitar out so that you can see the strings better, but this means your fingers have to curve more - if only about 10 degrees, it makes a lot of difference. I picked up this small tip recently and it is definitely producing cleaner chords, much less damping from fingers lying on strings.

After a few minutes you turn the chunks around and fry them on the other side for a few minutes. When this is done you've got some nice sear on largr parts of the meat. Now, chop up the meat chunks using a spatuala, fork or some other useful tool and also turn down the heat on the pan and put in some chunks of butter, spices, caramelized onions (whatever you like) and let it slow cook till done. That way the ground beef will be very tasty. GL!😊

These things are so cool. Growing up we had bleach and Nalgene bottles lol. Don't ever, ever assume water looks clean so it is clean, you'll regret it. I watched someone be sick with beaver fever before. That's giardiasis. 

You get beaver fever drinking water downstream from a dam or lodge. Oh man. Projectile. Out both ends. Poor guy was wishing for death he was so sick and stuff just kept coming out of him even though he was getting weak and struggled to get up. You only need to see that once to never, ever drink straight from a water source. 

If you don't have a life straw or something just put roughly 1 drop of bleach per 500 ml of water. Give it a really good shake and then let it sit before drinking it. If you have a canoe or go for a swim, get out into the deepest part of the water and scoop water an arm's length from the surface. It'll have the least amount of particulate. 

Do the same for cooking water. I  once ate a few bites of tuna helper someone made but they got the water from the shore. Full of sand. Disgusting lol. I went to bed hungry that night. If you can only access the shoreline then strain the water through a t-shirt or something first!

As a longtime bike mechanic, 15 years, the only reason you should switch out your Shimano brakes is to upgrade to a better Shimano brake. Sram are better then they use to be but still have a squishy modulation unless you over pressurize then when you bleed them. Other brakes have their own problems. Magura’s stop hard but are hard the work on and the lever body’s are plastic or carbon and have a tendency of breaking. Hopes are awesome but very expensive. Hayes work well but have a tendency to alway rub because the pistons don’t retract far enough. Tektro and TRP are very reliable and are very easy to work on but have awful brakes pads. They are usually to hard and don’t stop very well so i always recommend putting in Shimano brake pads or some other high end replacement brake pads. Formula brakes? If you enjoy riding bikes, just stay away from formula brakes. Hard to work on, bleed process is complicated, part are expensive. Brakes pads are expensive and feel terrible when braking not to mention that they are loud alway loud. Best brake out there is Shimano xt 8100 and xtr 9100.

My first interface was the Focusrite 2i2. Looking back, when you’re starting out, it really doesn’t matter which cheap interface you get, they all do the same basic job. The 2i2 is popular mostly because Focusrite did a great job with marketing. But here’s the thing: once you start noticing it’s not that great, don’t make the mistake (like me) of buying another “budget” interface that just costs a bit more. It’s not worth it.
If you’re serious and your music is starting to make you money, just invest in a proper pro interface. It’ll save you money and headaches in the long run.

As someone who can handstand quite well and has trained a couple people already, the tip with overcoming the fear is golden. Overcoming the fear will save begginers weeks or months of useless training. When you fear the position, you will literally gain zero balance troughout that period. You can overcome the fear in one session and get to the balancing. 

Another tip from me: Fuck progressions, they are useless. Just go straight to the balancing the real thing.

Another tip from me 2: Try to imagine you want to grab the ground. Squeeze and try to dip your fingers into the ground. Also train only on hard and level surfaces, soft surfaces will halt your progress as a begginer.

Another tip from me 3: When you feel like you have a plateau, two things work for most people - take a break, 3 days will do the trick, or try paralets. On paralets, you have more strength balancing with your arms and can focus on the rest of the body.

Another tip from me 4: When you can do 10 seconds consistently, start incorporating shapes such as straddle or tuck and also try lowering into an L-sit on paralets.

Another tip from me 5, and take this one very SERIOUSLY: Warm up your wrist every single time, it will not only help you with balancing but if something is off, it will warn you before you jump into the position and injure yourself for months. I got injured for 3 months practicing my 1-arm handstand because I though I can skip warmups. Also, if you already feel injured or your wrists hurt, train on paralets, they take most of the weight off and will be a great training tool for those times when your wrists are sore. Wrist wraps can also work in a pinch.


TLDR: Get paralets, dont do progressions (waste of time), squeeze the ground, play with your body shape, WARM YOUR WRISTS UP YOU LAZY BASTARD!

An old roommate of mine once met someone at the local game store. Turns out they had won a number of mini painting competitions in their day, so they were talking about painting minis and had asked him about his guardsman army. "These all have such personality to them. How long did it take you to paint them?"
"Two, maybe three hours?"
"That's quite a lot of work for each piece."
"No, that's for the whole army."
"... You painted this whole army in under three hours?"
"I mean, I go back after each game I play and add accoutrements that I feel each unit has earned, but, yeah."
"Teach me."
This is the method he uses. He also taught a mutual friend of ours how to paint. They went from utter novice to receiving commissions in under 3 months. Adderall was involved, so your mileage may vary, but it's definitely a way to understand the fundamentals of what makes a piece look good. You can also vary your printer layer to contain multiple colors to make an area appear lit or corrupted or cracked with energy. As such it's a great scaffold for learning more complex techniques, as well.

The tallest man in history was Robert Wadlow, who stood at 8 feet 11.1 inches tall. He was born on Feb. 22, 1918.

Wadlow's height came as a result of pituitary gigantism. By the time he was 5, Wadlow was 5 feet 4 inches tall, Guinness World Records reports. At 8 years old, he was nearly 6 feet tall.

Wadlow also holds the record for the largest feet and the largest hands ever. Wadlow's feet measured 18.5 inches long and his hands were 12.7 inches long from his wrist to the tip of his middle finger.

Wadlow died on July 15, 1940, due to an infection. He was 22 years old.

Limes actually mostly contain pectin in their skin, so you'd have much more of a jammy/stickier texture if you used something like grapefruits, lemons or oranges.
